name: coq au vin
instructions:
Classic french chicken-and-red-wine braise. Distinguished from the beef-and-mushroom version (see french-beef-and-mushroom-stew) by leaner protein and by leaning harder on lard + butter for depth — coq au vin doesn't have tomato-based umami like the beef variant, so the fat is doing more of the flavor work.

INGREDIENTS:
- chicken thighs with SKIN AND BONE (or ideally a whole chicken cut into pieces — see notes; boneless-skinless is a downgrade)
- LARDONS or cubed lard (see notes on lard vs butter ratio)
- BUTTER (unsalted, generous)
- pearl onions (or yellow onions, COARSELY CHOPPED — not fine mirepoix; see notes)
- carrots, coarsely chopped in ~1-inch chunks
- portabella mushrooms (or crimini), quartered
- garlic, minced
- RED WINE — PINOT NOIR (see notes; cabernet clashes)
- stock (chicken stock canonical)
- worcestershire sauce AND/OR a small dab of tamarind paste (for depth/umami)
- fresh thyme (canonical) and sage (see thyme/sage vs rosemary note)
- herbes de provence
- bay leaf
- all-purpose flour (for thickening — dredging chicken, or beurre manie at the end)
- salt, black pepper

PROCEDURE:
1. RENDER LARDONS: cube lard (or use pre-cut lardons) and render slowly in a heavy pot with a knob of butter to start. Crisp the lardons; set aside.
2. SEAR THE CHICKEN: dredge chicken pieces lightly in flour, salt, and pepper. Sear in the rendered lard-butter fat until deeply golden on all sides. Set aside.
3. DEEP SAUTÉ THE VEGETABLES — CARAMELIZE, DON'T STEAM: in the same fat, add the COARSELY-CHOPPED pearl onions (or chunky yellow onion), carrots, and mushrooms. Sauté in a hot pan long enough to get real color on the vegetables — you're not going for softened-and-translucent (that's mirepoix territory for a sauce base). You want browned edges and caramelized surfaces on the onion and carrot chunks. Add garlic when the vegetables are mostly there. This deep sauté is doing structural flavor work; don't rush it.
4. DEGLAZE WITH PINOT NOIR: pour in a generous glass of pinot noir; scrape up the fond. Reduce briefly.
5. BUILD THE BRAISE: add chicken stock (enough to nearly cover), worcestershire sauce (a splash) and/or a small dab of tamarind paste, thyme, sage, herbes de provence, and bay leaf. Return the chicken and lardons to the pot.
6. BRAISE: simmer covered on low ~45 min (thighs) up to 1.5 hours (whole chicken pieces bone-in) until chicken is fork-tender.
7. THICKEN (OPTIONAL): if the sauce needs body at the end, whisk in a beurre manie (butter kneaded with flour) off heat.
8. FINISH: another knob of butter stirred in at the very end for gloss. Salt and pepper to taste. Serve over crusty bread, mashed potatoes, or buttered egg noodles.
notes:
PINOT NOIR — NOT CABERNET: this is the single most important wine choice for coq au vin. Cabernet's bigger tannins and fruit-forward character read too jammy and overpowering in the sauce; the wine ends up dominating the dish and the chicken tastes like an afterthought inside a red-wine reduction. Pinot noir is lighter, more earthy-and-cherry, and integrates with the mushrooms and thyme rather than fighting them. A burgundy pinot noir is canonical (coq au vin was a Burgundy dish before it became a Julia Child dish). If not pinot, a light Beaujolais or a Chianti-style Sangiovese works in a pinch; not cabernet, not a big Napa red, not a syrah.

=== CHICKEN CUT — BONE-IN, SKIN-ON THIGHS OR IDEALLY A WHOLE CHICKEN ===

Use bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs at minimum; a whole chicken cut into pieces (thighs, drumsticks, breast, wings) is the ideal — this recipe is traditionally made from a whole rooster ("coq") and the mix of dark and white meat gives the finished stew textural interest. The bones enrich the braising liquid and the skin renders during the sear to add fat and flavor to the sauce. Boneless-skinless chicken breast is a real downgrade here — the meat dries out during the braise and you lose the bone-and-skin contribution to the sauce.

=== LARD AND BUTTER — BOTH LOAD-BEARING, MORE THAN IN THE BEEF VARIANT ===

LARD + BUTTER ARE MORE IMPORTANT HERE THAN IN THE BEEF VARIANT (SEE FRENCH-BEEF-AND-MUSHROOM-STEW): the french beef variant has tomato paste and worcestershire doing structural umami work; coq au vin doesn't get much umami support from its ingredient list beyond the mushrooms and the wine. That means the fat is doing more of the flavor lifting, and lard + butter both matter more here than there.

CURRENT RATIO — 2/3 BUTTER, 1/3 LARD; TRADITIONAL LEANS MORE LARD: I'm currently doing about 2/3 butter and 1/3 lard, which is delicious but butter-heavy compared to traditional coq au vin. The classical french recipe uses LARDONS (cubed cured pork fat/belly) as the fat backbone, and I think traditionally the ratio would be closer to 2/3 lard (via lardons) and 1/3 butter. Worth trying the traditional lean-lard direction as an experiment — the smoky-cured character of properly-crisped lardons is doing meaningful flavor work that pure lard alone doesn't quite match. Both fats always present; the exact ratio is a stylistic choice.

RENDER THE LARDONS SLOWLY FIRST: whether using pre-cured lardons or cubed lard, render them slowly at the start so they crisp without burning. Their rendered fat becomes part of the searing medium; the crisp lardons themselves get set aside and scattered back in at the end for texture.

=== HERB CHOICE — THYME AND SAGE PREFERRED; ROSEMARY USABLE IN RESTRAINT ===

THYME AND SAGE >> ROSEMARY IN COQ AU VIN: thyme is canonical here (a bouquet garni with fresh thyme is traditional), and sage pairs cleanly with the pinot noir and chicken. Rosemary is a common french-stew herb and it's not wrong here in small amounts, but it needs restraint — its piney assertiveness can dominate the pinot noir's delicate cherry-and-earth character. This is opposite of the beef variant, where rosemary is mandatory and generous. Use a light hand with rosemary here; lean on thyme and sage as the primary herb choices.

HERBES DE PROVENCE ROUNDS OUT THE HERB STACK: alongside thyme and sage, herbes de provence (which contains thyme, savory, marjoram, oregano, and sometimes lavender) fills in the aromatic background without any single herb dominating.

=== DEPTH / UMAMI — WORCESTERSHIRE OR TAMARIND ===

Coq au vin doesn't have tomato paste (that's the beef variant's move), so it needs its umami depth from elsewhere. Worcestershire sauce (a splash) and/or a small dab of tamarind paste fill that slot. Both share a tamarind-fermented-fruit-and-anchovy register that pairs well with the pinot noir and mushrooms. Use one or the other, or a small amount of both — don't stack in meaningful quantity.

=== NO TOMATO — SEE BEEF VARIANT FOR CONTRAST ===

Coq au vin has no tomato element at all. Tomato paste, canned tomatoes, fresh tomato — all wrong here. The french-beef-and-mushroom-stew uses tomato paste + a fresh chopped tomato as part of its umami structure; coq au vin does not have those ingredients and doesn't want them. If you find yourself reaching for tomato paste, make the beef variant instead.

=== NO POTATOES IN COQ AU VIN (UNLIKE THE BEEF VARIANT) ===

Coq au vin doesn't have potatoes in the braise. This is a major differentiator from french-beef-and-mushroom-stew, where potatoes ARE critical structural ingredients simmering in the pot. Potatoes here would absorb the pinot noir into a bland-starch register and dull the concentrated wine sauce that coq au vin is built around. Serve WITH mashed potatoes, buttered egg noodles, or crusty bread on the side if you want a starch component, but don't cook potatoes into the pot.

=== COARSE CHOP, DEEP SAUTÉ — NO MIREPOIX ===

This is a rustic braise, not a fine-sauce dish. Coarse-chop the onions, carrots, and mushrooms into visible chunks — you want to see and taste discrete vegetable pieces in the finished stew, not a dissolved-into-sauce mirepoix base. But: even though you're going coarse, you need to sauté HARD enough to get real caramelization on the vegetable surfaces before the wine and stock go in. Deep sauté phase does structural flavor work — the maillard browning on the carrot and onion chunks is doing umami lifting that the ingredient list doesn't give you from tomato (see NO TOMATO note). Rush the sauté and the finished stew reads flat.

=== DIFFERENTIATION FROM FRENCH-BEEF-AND-MUSHROOM-STEW ===

Both are french red-wine braises with mushrooms, carrots, and onions. Differences:
- PROTEIN: chicken vs beef. Chicken is leaner and cooks faster; braising time is 45 min to 1.5 hours vs the beef variant's longer braise. Coq au vin specifically wants bone-in skin-on chicken thighs or a whole chicken cut into pieces (see chicken cut note above); the beef variant uses ground beef by default.
- WINE: pinot noir here; the beef variant should also skip cabernet but tolerates a bigger red (still not cabernet — see beef recipe).
- TOMATO: coq au vin has none; beef variant has tomato paste + fresh tomato for umami depth.
- POTATOES: NONE in coq au vin (would absorb the pinot into bland starch); CRITICAL in the beef variant (structural starchy body simmering in the pot).
- FAT: lard + butter more critical here (no tomato umami to lean on); beef variant is more flexible on fat.
- HERBS: thyme + sage here (rosemary usable in restraint); rosemary + bay generous in beef. Rosemary can overpower coq au vin's pinot; thyme + sage would be too subtle for the beef.
- LARDONS: canonical here; optional in the beef variant.
- VEGETABLE CUT: both variants want COARSE chop with DEEP SAUTÉ / CARAMELIZATION, not fine-diced mirepoix. See sauté note above.
